title: Larkin-Ovchinnikov-Fulde-Ferrell state of spin polarized atomic Fermi superfluid on a spherical surface
abstract: By implementing the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) formalism of population-imbalanced atomic Fermi gases with pairing interactions in a thin spherical shell, we characterize the Larkin-Ovchinnikov-Fulde-Ferrell (LOFF) state in such a compact geometry. We first construct a phase diagram showing where uniform solutions of spin-polarized Fermi superfluid from the BdG equation cease to exist due to the vanishing order parameter. Near the boundary, various LOFF states with spatially modulating order parameters and density profiles can survive as convergent solutions to the BdG equation. When both uniform and LOFF solutions are present, we compare their grand potentials to determine the energetically favorable state and find that the LOFF states with multiple nodes in the order parameter become more stable at higher spin polarization. However, the LOFF state only survives close to the phase boundary where the uniform solutions vanish, indicating fragility of the LOFF state on a spherical surface. We also briefly discuss possible implications.
summary: This paper examines how the LOFF state behaves when a spin-polarized Fermi gas is placed on a spherical surface. It demonstrates that while spatial modulation can accommodate population imbalance, the LOFF phase is fragile and only stable near the breakdown of uniform superfluidity. The study highlights how compact, curved geometries influence the stability of inhomogeneous quantum phases.
main_problem: The study investigates the existence, stability, and spatial structure of the Larkin-Ovchinnikov-Fulde-Ferrell (LOFF) state in a population-imbalanced Fermi superfluid confined to a thin spherical shell.
main_result: The researchers found that LOFF states with multiple nodes can become energetically favorable at high spin polarization, though the phase is fragile and exists only near the boundary where uniform superfluidity vanishes.
method: The Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) formalism is used, expanding quasiparticle wavefunctions in spherical harmonics and solving the resulting matrix equations iteratively.
model_or_system: A two-component, spin-polarized atomic Fermi gas with pairing interactions confined to a 2D thin spherical surface, modeled using a BCS mean-field Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Order parameter (gap function) spatial modulation, spin-resolved densities, total density profiles, and the grand potential.
important_parameters: Spin polarization (p), interaction strength (-ln(k_F a)), chemical potentials, and angular momentum quantum number (l).
main_assumptions: The study assumes zero temperature (T=0), contact pairing interactions, the BCS mean-field approximation, and azimuthal symmetry.
figures_summary: Figures show phase boundaries for uniform vs. non-uniform solutions, spatial profiles of the order parameter and densities for various LOFF states, and energy comparisons of the grand potential across different polarizations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of LOFF in curved geometry, describes the BdG formalism and spherical expansion, presents the phase diagram and stability analysis, details the spatial structure of LOFF nodes, and concludes with a discussion on the fragility of the state.
why_interesting: This work is relevant to many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it explores how geometric curvature and topological constraints influence the formation of inhomogeneous superfluid phases in ultracold atomic gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Uniform solutions showing (a) µ, (b) h, and (c) ∆as functions of −ln(kF a) at T = 0. The solid, dash, and dotted lines are for p = 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5, respectively. No uniform solution can be found beyond the point when ∆→0.

FIG. 3. Panels (a),(c),(e): Order parameter ∆/EF as a function of cos θ. Panels (b),(d),(f): Spin-resolved densities n↑/n0 (black solid lines), n↓/n0 (red dashed lines), and to- tal density n/n0 (blue dot-dash lines) as functions of cos θ, where n0 = N/(4π). For (a) and (b), −ln(kF a) = −0.81, p = 0.1 and µ↑/EF = 1.4, µ↓/EF = 0.56. For (c) and (d), −ln(kF a) = −0.78 and p = 0.14, µ↑,↓are the same as (a) and (b). For (e) and (f), −ln(kF a) = −0.93, p = 0.3 and µ↑/EF = 1.6, µ↓/EF = 0.53. For all cases, T = 0.

FIG. 4. The expansion coeﬃcients al of the LOFF order pa- rameter according to Eq. (27) as a function of l. The red circles, blue squares, and green triangles correspond to the one-, two-, and three-node LOFF states of Fig. 3, respec- tively.

FIG. 5. The normalized grand potential per particle (E/N)/EF of spin-polarized superﬂuid as a function of −ln(kF a) at T = 0. Here p = 0.1, 0.14, 0.3 from top to bot- tom, which correspond to the cases shown in Fig. 3. The red lines represent the uniform states. The black triangles are selected LOFF states with one, two, and three nodes in the order parameter when the polarization increases. Only the three-node LOFF state in the bottom is energetically more favorable than the uniform solution.

FIG. 6. (Left) The order parameter ∆/EF as a function of cos θ. (Right) The spin-resolved densities n↑/n0 (black solid line), n↓/n0 (red dashed line), and the total density n/n0 (blue dot-dash line) as functions of cos θ, where n0 = N/(4π). Here −ln(kF a) = −0.81, p = 0.43, µ↑/EF = 1.78, µ↓/EF = 0.35, and T = 0.
